walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Pandemic: Hot Zone set up. First, unfold the compact game board and place it in the center of the table where everyone can reach it. This board shows a map of North America with twenty-four cities connected by lines. You will see three colored regions — blue in the north, yellow in the south, and red in the west. These colors match the three diseases you need to cure.

walkthrough.title: Place the Markers
walkthrough.text: Now let's set up the tracking markers on the board. Find the infection rate marker — it is a small round token. Place it on the first space of the infection rate track, which shows the number two. This means you will draw two infection cards at the end of each turn to start. Next, find the outbreak marker and place it on the zero space of the outbreak track. If this marker ever reaches eight, everyone loses.

walkthrough.title: Sort the Disease Cubes
walkthrough.text: Find all twenty-four disease cubes. They come in three colors — eight blue, eight yellow, and eight red. Each color represents a different disease spreading across North America. Sort them by color and place them in three piles near the board where everyone can reach them. These cubes will be placed on cities as diseases spread. If you ever need to place a cube and there are none left of that color, everyone loses immediately.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Cure Markers
walkthrough.text: Find the three cure markers — one for each disease color. Place them near the board with the vial side face down. When your team discovers a cure for a disease, you will flip the matching marker to show the vial. You need to flip all three to win the game. Curing a disease does not remove cubes from the board — it just means the Medic becomes more powerful against that color and treating becomes easier.

walkthrough.title: Infect the Starting Cities
walkthrough.text: Now let's seed the initial infections. Shuffle the infection deck — these are the cards with city names that match the board. Draw three cards and place three cubes of the matching color on each of those cities. Then draw three more cards and place two cubes on each. Then draw three final cards and place one cube on each. That is nine cities infected to start. Place all drawn infection cards face up in the infection discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Player Deck
walkthrough.text: Shuffle all twenty-four city cards together. Deal starting hands based on player count — four cards each for two players, three cards each for three players, or two cards each for four players. Now divide the remaining city cards into equal piles matching the number of epidemic cards you want to use. For your first game, use three epidemic cards for an easier experience. Shuffle one epidemic card into each pile without looking, then stack the piles on top of each other. This is your player deck.

walkthrough.title: Choose Roles
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the role cards and deal one to each player, or let everyone pick their favorite. Each role has a unique special ability printed on the card. Read your ability out loud so the whole team knows what everyone can do. Place your role card face up in front of you and take the matching colored pawn. Every role changes how you play, so understanding your teammates' abilities is crucial for coordination.

walkthrough.title: Place Pawns and Begin
walkthrough.text: Place all player pawns on Atlanta — that is your starting city and home base. Everyone begins together, which is a nice head start for coordination. Pick a first player however you like — maybe whoever was sick most recently. Play goes clockwise from there. You are ready to save North America. Let's learn the rules.

walkthrough.title: How You Win and Lose
walkthrough.text: Pandemic: Hot Zone is a cooperative game — everyone wins or loses together. You win by discovering cures for all three diseases. You do not need to remove every cube from the board, just find the cures. You lose if any of three things happen: the outbreak marker reaches eight, you cannot place a disease cube because that color's supply is empty, or a player cannot draw two cards from the player deck. Keep all three loss conditions in mind as you play.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Each player's turn has three phases, always in the same order. First, take four actions. You can mix and match from the available action types in any combination — even repeat the same action four times. Second, draw two cards from the player deck. If you draw an epidemic card, resolve it immediately. Third, infect cities by drawing cards from the infection deck equal to the current infection rate and adding one cube to each city drawn. Then the next player goes.

walkthrough.title: Movement Actions
walkthrough.text: You have three ways to move around the board. Drive or Ferry lets you move to any city connected by a line to your current city — this is free and costs no cards. Direct Flight lets you discard a city card from your hand to fly directly to that city, no matter where it is on the board. Charter Flight lets you discard the card matching your current city to fly to any city on the board. Drive is the most common, but sometimes you need to spend a card to get across the map quickly.

walkthrough.title: Treat Disease
walkthrough.text: The Treat Disease action lets you remove one disease cube from the city you are currently in. Just pick up one cube of any color present in that city and return it to the supply pile. If the disease of that color has already been cured, treating is supercharged — you remove all cubes of that color from the city instead of just one. Treating does not cure the disease, but it prevents outbreaks and buys your team time.

walkthrough.title: Share Knowledge
walkthrough.text: The Share Knowledge action lets two players exchange a city card, but there is a strict rule — the card traded must match the city both players are currently standing in. So if you and a teammate are both in Miami, you can give or take the Miami card. This is one of the trickiest actions to coordinate because it requires being in the same city at the same time with the right card. Plan your movements together to set up these trades.

walkthrough.title: Discover a Cure
walkthrough.text: This is how you win the game. To discover a cure, you must be at a research station and discard four city cards of the same color. That is it — four matching cards and a research station. The cure marker for that color gets flipped to show the vial. Once a disease is cured, treating it becomes more efficient, but cubes of that color can still spread. You need to cure all three colors to win.

walkthrough.title: Drawing Player Cards
walkthrough.text: After your four actions, draw two cards from the player deck and add them to your hand. City cards are your most valuable resource — they are used for flying, sharing knowledge, and discovering cures, so every card matters. There is a hand limit of seven cards. If you ever have more than seven cards in your hand at the end of your turn, you must discard down to seven immediately.

walkthrough.title: Epidemic Cards
walkthrough.text: When you draw an epidemic card, bad things happen in three steps. First, Increase — move the infection rate marker up one space on the track, which means more cities get infected each turn from now on. Second, Infect — draw the bottom card of the infection deck and place three cubes of the matching color on that city. Third, Intensify — shuffle all the cards in the infection discard pile and place them on top of the infection deck. This means cities that were already infected will get hit again soon.

walkthrough.title: Infecting Cities
walkthrough.text: The last phase of your turn is infecting cities. Draw cards from the infection deck equal to the current infection rate — that starts at two but increases with epidemics. For each card drawn, add one disease cube of the matching color to that city. If the city already has cubes of a different color, you still add the new color. Then place the drawn infection cards in the discard pile. This steady drip of infection is what creates the tension — you are always racing against the spread.

walkthrough.title: Outbreaks
walkthrough.text: If a city would ever receive a fourth cube of the same color, an outbreak occurs instead. Do not place the fourth cube. Instead, move the outbreak marker up one space and add one cube of that color to every city connected by a line to the outbreak city. If any of those connected cities also reach four cubes of the same color, a chain reaction outbreak happens. Each city can only outbreak once per chain, but chain reactions can be devastating. Eight total outbreaks and everyone loses.

walkthrough.title: Role Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each player's role card gives them a unique power that bends or breaks the normal rules. The Medic removes all cubes of one color as a single Treat Disease action instead of just one cube. The Researcher can give any city card during Share Knowledge, not just the one matching their current city. Read each role card carefully — these abilities are incredibly powerful when used well. Coordinate with your team to make the most of every role at the table.

walkthrough.title: Putting It All Together
walkthrough.text: Here is the rhythm of the game. On your turn, take four actions — move around, treat diseases, trade cards, or discover cures. Then draw two cards, hoping for the colors you need and dreading epidemics. Then infect cities and watch the diseases creep closer to outbreak. Talk to your team constantly. Plan your card trades. Decide together who should go where. The game is a puzzle, and you solve it by communicating. Cure all three diseases and you win together.

summary.title: How You Win and Lose
bullets: WIN: Cure all 3 diseases (don't need to remove all cubes)
LOSE: 8 outbreaks, OR cube supply runs out, OR can't draw 2 player cards

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of Pandemic: Hot Zone! In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the first few turns of a three-player game. We will use three example players — Player 1 is the Medic, Player 2 is the Researcher, and Player 3 is the Generalist. Everyone starts in Atlanta. Just follow along with the moves, even if your roles or player count differ.

walkthrough.title: Read the Board Before You Move
walkthrough.text: Before anyone takes a turn, let's read the board together. Look at the nine cities that were infected during setup. Which cities have three cubes? Those are the most dangerous — one more cube and they will outbreak. Which color has the most cubes on the board? That disease is the most urgent threat right now. Talk about it as a team. In Pandemic, the board tells you where to go.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 (Medic): Rush to a Hotspot
walkthrough.text: Player 1, you are the Medic — the best disease fighter on the team. Look at the board and find the closest city with three cubes. Use your first actions to drive toward it. If it is two cities away, spend two actions driving there, then use your third action to Treat Disease and remove one cube. Use your fourth action to treat again, removing another cube. That city just went from three cubes to one — much safer now.

walkthrough.title: Why the Medic Goes First
walkthrough.text: We sent the Medic to the hotspot because the Medic is the most efficient at treating disease. Once a disease is cured later in the game, the Medic removes all cubes of that color in one action. But even before any cures, the Medic should be the firefighter — rushing to three-cube cities to prevent outbreaks while other players focus on collecting cards for cures.

walkthrough.title: Player 1 Draws Cards
walkthrough.text: Now Player 1 draws two cards from the player deck. Let's say they draw a blue city card and a yellow city card. Add both to your hand. Good news — no epidemic this time. Then infect cities by drawing cards equal to the infection rate, which is two. Add one cube to each city drawn. Check if either city already had three cubes. If so, an outbreak would happen. Hopefully not this early.

walkthrough.title: Player 2 (Researcher): Start Collecting Cards
walkthrough.text: Player 2, you are the Researcher. Your special power is that you can give any city card to another player in your city, not just the card matching your location. This makes you the best card trader on the team. For now, drive toward a cluster of infected cities. Use three actions to move and one action to treat a nearby two-cube city down to one cube.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Ignoring Card Colors
walkthrough.text: After drawing cards, Player 2 looks at their hand — two blue cards, one red card, and one yellow card. They have no plan for which color to collect. Here is the mistake: they are not thinking about cures yet. In Pandemic, you need four cards of the same color to discover a cure. If nobody on the team is working toward a color, those cards will sit useless in your hand until the deck runs out and you lose.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Assign Colors Early
walkthrough.text: Right now, stop and look at everyone's hands. Who has the most cards of each color? Assign each player a color to collect. Maybe Player 1 focuses on yellow, Player 2 focuses on blue, and Player 3 focuses on red. This does not mean you ignore other colors — it means you have a plan for who trades cards to whom. Without this plan, cards scatter randomly and you will never get four of a kind.

walkthrough.title: Player 3: The Mistake of Solo Wandering
walkthrough.text: Player 3 looks at the board and decides to drive across the map alone to treat a single-cube city on the far side. They spend all four actions moving and do not treat anything because they ran out of actions before arriving. Meanwhile, the hotspot cities near Atlanta are still dangerous. This was a wasted turn — Player 3 spent their entire turn traveling to a low-priority target and accomplished nothing useful.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Stay Near Your Team
walkthrough.text: In Pandemic, you need to be near other players to share knowledge and trade cards. A player who wanders off alone cannot participate in card trades, which are essential for discovering cures. Stick together in loose clusters. Treat nearby hotspots, but always keep the team close enough that you can meet up for a card trade within one or two turns.

walkthrough.title: An Epidemic Hits
walkthrough.text: On the next draw phase, someone draws an epidemic card. Do not panic. Follow the three steps. First, move the infection rate marker up one notch — it now shows three instead of two, which means you will draw three infection cards at the end of each turn from now on. Second, draw the bottom card of the infection deck and place three cubes on that city. Third, shuffle the infection discard pile and put it on top of the infection deck. This is the scariest part — cities that were already infected are about to get infected again.

walkthrough.title: Post-Epidemic: Predict the Danger
walkthrough.text: After the Intensify step, the cities in the infection discard pile are now on top of the infection deck. That means the cities that were infected during setup — especially the ones with three cubes — are coming back around. This is the most important moment to communicate with your team. Which of those cities still have two or three cubes? Those are your outbreak risks. Send someone there now before it is too late.

walkthrough.title: The Mistake: Treating Instead of Curing
walkthrough.text: Several turns in, the team has been spending all their actions treating disease and none on working toward cures. The board looks manageable, but nobody has more than two cards of any color. Here is the trap: treating disease is reactive. You are putting out fires but never solving the underlying problem. Every turn you spend treating instead of moving toward a cure is a turn closer to the deck running out.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Balance Treating and Curing
walkthrough.text: You cannot ignore the board — three-cube cities will outbreak if left untreated. But you also cannot ignore cures — that is the only way to win. The balance is this: treat only what is truly dangerous, meaning cities with three cubes or cities that are about to be re-infected after an epidemic. Spend every other action moving toward card trades and cures. One player should always be actively working toward the next cure.

walkthrough.title: Setting Up a Card Trade
walkthrough.text: Let's practice a card trade. Player 2 the Researcher has two blue cards and wants to give one to Player 1. Remember, the Researcher's special ability lets them give any card, not just the one matching their city. Player 2 needs to be in the same city as Player 1. So this turn, Player 2 drives to Player 1's city and uses the Share Knowledge action to hand over a blue card. That is two blue cards toward a cure now in one player's hand.

walkthrough.title: Racing Toward the First Cure
walkthrough.text: After a few more turns of strategic trading, Player 2 has collected four blue city cards. Now they need to get to Atlanta, which has a research station. Player 2 spends actions driving back to Atlanta and uses one action to Discover a Cure, discarding all four blue cards. Flip the blue cure marker. One down, two to go. Now the Medic can remove all blue cubes from any city in a single action — the blue disease is under control.

walkthrough.title: Continue Playing
walkthrough.text: You have seen the core loop in action — treat hotspots, collect cards, trade with teammates, and discover cures. From here, keep playing on your own. Focus on the next color to cure. Remember to check the board after every epidemic and predict which cities are about to get re-infected. Use your role powers aggressively. Communicate every turn. When all three cures are discovered, you win together.

walkthrough.title: Before You Save the World
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First — check out General Tips for strategy basics and the most common mistakes to avoid.

Second — reference the Appendix for a quick guide to actions, epidemic steps, and game terms.

And most importantly — ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Good luck out there!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we get into strategy, here are the most common mistakes new players make. First, playing solo instead of cooperating — Pandemic is won through card trades, which require being near your teammates. Second, treating every single cube instead of focusing on three-cube cities about to outbreak. Third, ignoring which color cards each player is collecting, leading to scattered hands that never reach four of a kind. Fourth, forgetting to predict the infection deck after an epidemic reshuffles the discard pile back on top.

walkthrough.title: Assign Cure Responsibilities
walkthrough.text: At the start of the game, look at everyone's hands and decide who will work toward which color cure. If Player 1 has two blue cards, assign blue to them. The rest of the team should funnel blue cards their way through Share Knowledge trades. It is much faster to have one player collect four matching cards than to have three players each holding scattered colors. Reassess assignments as new cards are drawn — sometimes the deck decides for you.

walkthrough.title: Prioritize Hotspots After Epidemics
walkthrough.text: The most dangerous moment in Pandemic is right after an epidemic. When the infection discard pile gets shuffled and placed on top of the deck, every city that was previously infected is about to get hit again. Cities with two or three cubes are now at extreme risk of outbreak. After every epidemic, stop and scan the board. Which cities have three cubes and are in the infection discard pile you just shuffled back on top? Send someone there immediately.

walkthrough.title: Use Card-Based Movement Wisely
walkthrough.text: Every city card you discard for travel is a card you cannot use for a cure. Direct Flight and Charter Flight are powerful, but they cost you a cure component. Use them only when the situation is truly urgent — like preventing an outbreak that would cascade into multiple cities. Most of the time, driving one city at a time is the better play. Plan your routes two or three turns ahead so you can drive instead of flying.

walkthrough.title: Lean on Role Abilities
walkthrough.text: Role powers are extremely strong in this game, and new players often forget to use them. The Medic should always be heading toward the most infected area. The Researcher should be actively meeting teammates to pass cards. Read your role card again right now. If you are not using your special ability at least once every couple of turns, you are probably not playing your role aggressively enough. Your role defines your job on the team.

walkthrough.title: Do Not Waste Actions on Low-Priority Cubes
walkthrough.text: A city with one cube is not a crisis. A city with two cubes is worth watching. A city with three cubes is an emergency. Focus your Treat Disease actions on three-cube cities, especially those that appeared in the infection discard pile and will get drawn again soon. Treating a single cube in a quiet corner of the map while a three-cube city is about to outbreak is one of the most common ways new teams lose.

walkthrough.title: Manage the Hand Limit
walkthrough.text: The seven-card hand limit is easy to forget and painful when it catches you off guard. If you have six cards and draw two, you must immediately discard down to seven. That wasted card could have been a cure component. Pay attention to hand sizes before drawing. If a teammate is close to the limit, try to set up a Share Knowledge trade before their next draw phase so they can pass a useful card instead of discarding it.

walkthrough.title: Think Two Turns Ahead
walkthrough.text: The best Pandemic teams plan multiple turns in advance. Before taking your turn, say out loud what you intend to do and ask if anyone has a better idea. Think about where you need to be in two turns for a card trade. Think about which cities are likely to get infected based on the infection discard pile. If you can predict where the danger will be, you can prevent outbreaks before they happen. The team that plans together wins together.

bullets: Don't play solo — card trades require proximity to teammates
Don't treat every cube — focus on 3-cube cities about to outbreak
Assign each player a color to collect or cards scatter uselessly
After epidemics, predict which cities will get re-infected

summary.title: Do Not Waste Actions on Low-Priority Cubes

Treat 3-cube cities first, especially those in the infection discard
Treating quiet corners while hotspots brew is how teams lose
Let low-cube cities wait unless they're about to be re-infected

summary.title: Manage the Hand Limit

Watch hand sizes before draw phase to avoid waste
Set up trades before hitting the limit — pass cards, don't discard them

summary.title: Think Two Turns Ahead
bullets: Announce your plan out loud — ask team for input
Plan card trades 2 turns ahead — where do you need to meet?
Predict infections from the discard pile after epidemics
The team that plans together wins together

walkthrough.title: Cure Order Strategy
walkthrough.text: Not all diseases are equally dangerous. At the start of the game, look at which color has the most cubes on the board and which cities have three cubes. Ideally, cure the most dangerous disease first because curing it makes the Medic incredibly efficient against that color. However, there is a wrinkle — sometimes you should cure the color you already have the most cards for, even if it is not the most dangerous. Getting one cure early is a huge momentum boost. Assess both factors: urgency on the board versus card availability in your hands.

walkthrough.title: Researcher Card Pipeline
walkthrough.text: The Researcher is the single most powerful role for discovering cures quickly. Because they can give any card during Share Knowledge — not just the card matching their city — the Researcher should be the team's card hub. The optimal play is to have the Researcher collect cards normally, then meet up with the player assigned to that color and dump multiple cards at once. A well-timed Researcher rendezvous can enable a cure in a single turn. Always plan the Researcher's movement around card delivery.

walkthrough.title: Epidemic Probability Management
walkthrough.text: After each epidemic, you know exactly which cities are on top of the infection deck — they are the ones that were in the discard pile. You can count them and calculate the rough probability of any city being drawn in the next few turns. If a three-cube city is in that reshuffled stack and there are only five cards on top, there is a very high chance it gets drawn within two turns. Use this information to decide whether to rush there or treat nearby cities first. The infection deck is not random after an epidemic — it is almost predictable.

walkthrough.title: Controlled Outbreak Management
walkthrough.text: Sometimes an outbreak is inevitable and it is better to let it happen in a safe location than to waste actions preventing it. If a three-cube city is surrounded by cities with zero or one cube, an outbreak there adds one cube to each neighbor — inconvenient but not catastrophic. Compare that to a three-cube city surrounded by other two- and three-cube cities, where an outbreak would trigger a deadly chain reaction. Prioritize preventing chain outbreaks over preventing isolated ones.

walkthrough.title: Action Efficiency and Tempo
walkthrough.text: Every action in Pandemic has an opportunity cost. Driving to an adjacent city costs one action. Treating one cube costs one action. But discovering a cure — the only way to win — also costs one action plus four cards. The most efficient turns are ones where every action advances you toward a cure. Think of it like a budget: you have four actions per turn, and each one spent treating or driving is one fewer for cure progress. The best players minimize wasted actions by planning multi-turn routes that combine movement, treatment, and trading into the fewest possible turns.

walkthrough.title: Defensive Positioning
walkthrough.text: Instead of chasing outbreaks reactively, position your team defensively near high-risk clusters. After an epidemic, you know which cities will be infected next. Place one player near the densest cluster of high-cube cities so they can treat on their turn before the infection phase. The Medic is ideal for this — park them in a central location between multiple hotspots so they can drive one space to treat whichever city gets drawn. This proactive positioning prevents outbreaks before they happen.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Acceleration
walkthrough.text: When two diseases are cured and you are working on the third, shift into pure acceleration mode. Stop treating any disease that is already cured unless an outbreak is imminent — the Medic can handle those incidentally. Every other player should be focused entirely on collecting the fourth set of matching cards and getting to a research station. The player deck is running out, and every turn matters. If you can see the finish line, sprint for it.

walkthrough.title: Difficulty Scaling and Mastery
walkthrough.text: Once you win consistently with three epidemic cards, add the fourth. The jump from three to four epidemics is significant — the infection rate climbs faster, outbreaks are harder to prevent, and timing becomes much tighter. With four epidemics, every principle in this guide matters more. Card trades must be planned further ahead. Hotspot management becomes critical. Role abilities must be used to their fullest. Mastering the four-epidemic game is the ultimate challenge in Pandemic: Hot Zone.

summary.title: Cure Order Strategy
bullets: Cure the most dangerous color first (most cubes, most 3-cube cities)
BUT: if another color is close to 4 cards, cure that first for momentum
Getting 1 cure early boosts Medic efficiency enormously
Balance board urgency vs. card availability

summary.title: Researcher Card Pipeline
bullets: Researcher can give ANY card — not just the city they're in
Use Researcher as the team's card delivery hub
Plan multi-card dumps: Researcher meets target player, passes 2-3 cards
A good Researcher rendezvous can enable a cure in 1 turn

summary.title: Epidemic Probability Management
bullets: After epidemic, reshuffled discard is now on top of infection deck
You know exactly which cities are in that stack
Count cards: 3-cube city in a 5-card stack = very high outbreak risk
Infection deck is nearly predictable post-epidemic — use that info

summary.title: Controlled Outbreak Management
bullets: Not all outbreaks are equal — isolated outbreaks are survivable
3-cube city with 0-1 cube neighbors = low-damage outbreak
3-cube city with 2-3 cube neighbors = chain reaction = catastrophic
Prioritize preventing chain outbreaks over isolated ones

summary.title: Action Efficiency and Tempo
bullets: 4 actions per turn — each one spent treating = 1 fewer for cure progress
Best turns: every action advances toward a cure
Plan multi-turn routes combining movement, treatment, and trading
Minimize wasted actions by thinking 2-3 turns ahead

summary.title: Defensive Positioning
bullets: Position players near high-risk clusters proactively
After epidemic, you know which cities get infected next
Park Medic centrally between hotspots for 1-move treatment reach
Prevent outbreaks before they happen instead of chasing them

summary.title: Endgame Acceleration
bullets: 2 cures down → shift to pure acceleration for the 3rd
Stop treating cured diseases unless outbreak is imminent
Every player except Medic should focus on cards and trades
Player deck is running out — sprint for the last cure

summary.title: Difficulty Scaling and Mastery
bullets: Beat 3 epidemics consistently → add the 4th
4 epidemics: tighter timing, faster infection rate, harder outbreaks
All strategies become more critical at higher difficulty
Mastering 4-epidemic mode is the ultimate Hot Zone challenge

entries.definition: Move your pawn to an adjacent city connected by a line on the board. This is the basic free movement action and does not cost any cards. Most common action in the game.
Discard a city card from your hand to move your pawn directly to the city shown on that card, regardless of distance. Costs one card and one action.
Discard the city card matching your current location to fly to any city on the board. More flexible than Direct Flight but requires the specific card matching where you currently stand.

Remove one disease cube of any color from the city you are in. If that disease has been cured, remove all cubes of that color instead of just one. Does not cure the disease — only Discover a Cure does that.
Give or take a city card with another player. Both players must be in the same city, and the card exchanged must match that city. The Researcher role bypasses this restriction when giving cards.

At a research station, discard four city cards of the same color to cure that disease. Flip the matching cure marker. This is the only way to make progress toward winning. You do not need to remove all cubes — just find the cure.

A card shuffled into the player deck that triggers three devastating steps when drawn: Increase the infection rate, Infect a city with three cubes from the bottom of the infection deck, and Intensify by shuffling the infection discard pile back on top of the infection deck.

The main cards in the player deck. Each shows a city name and color matching one of the three disease regions. Used for Direct Flight, Charter Flight, Share Knowledge, and Discover a Cure. The most versatile and valuable resource in the game.

Cards drawn at the end of each turn from the infection deck. Each shows a city name and color. When drawn, one disease cube of that color is added to the named city. After epidemics, the discard pile is shuffled back on top, making previously infected cities get hit again.
The first step of an epidemic. Move the infection rate marker forward one space on the infection rate track. This permanently increases the number of infection cards drawn at the end of each turn.
The second step of an epidemic. Draw the bottom card of the infection deck and place three disease cubes of the matching color on that city. If this would cause a fourth cube, an outbreak occurs.

The third and most dangerous step of an epidemic. Shuffle all cards in the infection discard pile and place them face down on top of the infection deck. This means cities that were recently infected will be drawn again soon.

Triggered when a city would receive a fourth cube of the same color. The fourth cube is not placed. Instead, every city connected by a line receives one cube of that color. Chain reactions occur if connected cities also hit four cubes. Move the outbreak marker up one space per outbreak. Eight outbreaks total means the game is lost.

When an outbreak spreads cubes to a connected city that already has three cubes of that color, that city also outbreaks. Each city can only outbreak once per chain reaction. A single chain can trigger multiple outbreaks and rapidly fill the outbreak track.

The number of infection cards drawn at the end of each player's turn. Starts at two and increases each time an epidemic card is drawn. Tracked by the infection rate marker on the board. Higher rates mean faster disease spread.

A location where players can discover cures. In Pandemic: Hot Zone, Atlanta starts with a research station. Players must be at a research station and discard four matching city cards to cure a disease.
Players may hold a maximum of seven cards. If you exceed this limit at any point, you must immediately discard down to seven. Choose carefully — discarded cards cannot be recovered.

A disease that has had its cure discovered. The cure marker is flipped to the vial side. Cured diseases still spread normally, but the Treat Disease action removes all cubes of that color from a city instead of just one. Curing all three diseases wins the game.

A role that removes all cubes of one color from a city with a single Treat Disease action, even before that disease is cured. After a disease is cured, the Medic automatically removes cubes of that color from any city they enter or are in — no action required.

A role that can give any city card to another player in the same city during Share Knowledge, not just the card matching the current city. This makes the Researcher the most efficient card trader and a critical role for assembling cures quickly.

A role that gets five actions per turn instead of the normal four. The extra action adds up over the course of the game, giving the Generalist more flexibility to treat, move, and trade in a single turn.

A role that can move other players' pawns on their turn as an action. The Dispatcher can also move any pawn to any city containing another pawn. Extremely powerful for setting up Share Knowledge trades without the other player spending actions to travel.
